perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and for the purposes of the Privacy Code and EU Reg. 679/2016 ("GDPR") ("Law") on "Protection of persons and other subjects with regard to the processing of personal data", the Parties - as autonomous Data Controllers - acknowledge that they have exchanged information on the use of their personal data. The Parties undertake to communicate to each other - in execution of this Contract - only the common and/or sensitive personal data of third parties to whom they have given prior information and from whom they have previously acquired (where necessary) their -16- consent, in accordance with the Privacy Code. and EU Reg. 679/2016 ("GDPR") In particular, such consent must be informed, expressed, specific; documented in writing, in the case of common data; given in writing under penalty of nullity, in the case of sensitive data. Each Party shall be individually responsible for any communication of common and/or sensitive data made without the prior fulfilment of the aforementioned obligations. The Party to whom the communication is addressed will therefore be released from any responsibility and/or claim of third parties, related to the possible communication of common and/or sensitive data made in breach of the provisions of this clause and the Privacy Code and EU Reg. 679/2016.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to and for the purposes of the Privacy Code and EU Reg. 679/2016 ("GDPR") ("Law") on "Protection of persons and other subjects with regard to the processing of personal data", the Parties - as autonomous Data Controllers - acknowledge that they have exchanged information on the use of their personal data. The Parties undertake to communicate to each other - in execution of this Contract - common and/or sensitive personal data of third parties. Each Party shall be individually responsible for any communication of common and/or sensitive data made.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for prior informed consent for sharing personal data, directly contradicting GDPR regulations regarding data protection and privacy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1)(a) of the GDPR requires explicit consent for processing personal data. By removing the obligation to obtain prior informed consent, the modified text violates this fundamental principle of GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shall be determined in that legal basis or, as regards the processing referred to in point (e) of paragraph 1, shall be necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 3 That legal basis may contain specific provisions to adapt the application of rules of this Regulation, inter alia: the general conditions governing the lawfulness of processing by the controller; the types of data which are subject to the processing; the data subjects concerned; the entities to, and the purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed; the purpose limitation; storage periods; and processing operations and processing procedures, including measures to ensure lawful and fair processing such as those for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 4 The Union or the Member State law shall meet an objective of public interest and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law which constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard the objectives referred to in Article 23 (1), the controller shall, in order to ascertain whether processing for another purpose is compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected, take into account, inter alia: any link between the purposes for which the personal data have been collected and
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for prior informed consent before sharing personal data, which directly contradicts GDPR Article 6(1)(a) that mandates explicit consent for processing personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Art. 6 GDPR, states that processing is lawful only if 'the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes'. The removal of the prior consent requirement creates a clear violation, as it allows for data processing without the data subject's explicit agreement, making the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: 11. SENSITIVE INFORMATION The Parties shall keep confidential any information exchanged between them in connection with the negotiation, execution and performance of this JV Agreement; it is agreed that these confidentiality obligations shall not apply with respect to any information which: (a) becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of an unauthorised disclosure by a Party, (b) was available to a Party prior to its disclosure by the other Party, (c) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a court or other public authority or for the purpose of enforcing the rights and obligations set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. SENSITIVE INFORMATION The Parties shall keep confidential any information exchanged between them in connection with the negotiation, execution and performance of this JV Agreement; it is agreed that these confidentiality obligations shall not apply with respect to any information which: (a) becomes generally available to the public, (b) was available to a Party prior to its disclosure by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of clause (c), which allows disclosure when required by a court or public authority, creates a legal risk. This could potentially put a party in contempt of court if they are legally compelled to disclose information covered by the confidentiality agreement. This section also has implications for compliance with securities law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 78u-4 outlines procedures for private securities litigation. Under this section, parties may be compelled by a court to disclose information. By removing the provision that allows disclosure pursuant to a court order, the modified contract language creates a conflict with obligations to comply with valid legal orders, exposing a party to legal risks if they adhere to the confidentiality clause rather than a court order.

perturbation.justification: The omission of clause (c) in the changed text directly contradicts legal obligations by potentially preventing a party from disclosing information under court order, as highlighted in the perturbation explanation and law explanation. The original text acknowledges exceptions to confidentiality, specifically permitting disclosure when required by a court or public authority. The modified text removes this exception, creating a scenario where adhering to the confidentiality agreement could lead to contempt of court or other legal repercussions. The scraped snippets, while not explicitly detailing the exception, refer to securities litigation and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, implying potential legal obligations to disclose information under certain circumstances. Therefore, removing the provision for court-ordered disclosure directly creates a legal risk of non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall provide civil liability insurance cover to their own personnel with respect to accidents and damages charged to them.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may provide civil liability insurance cover to their own personnel with respect to accidents and damages charged to them.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may,' the requirement for civil liability insurance is removed. This is a contradiction because the absence of mandatory insurance coverage for staff creates a potential liability risk, which might violate standard labor laws requiring employers to ensure a safe working environment or compensate for damages caused to employees.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2087 of the Italian Civil Code mandates employers to adopt measures that protect the physical integrity and moral personality of workers. Removing mandatory civil liability insurance can leave employees without recourse in case of work-related accidents or damages, thus contradicting the employer's responsibility under Italian law to provide a safe working environment and protect employee well-being.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' removes a mandatory requirement for civil liability insurance. The Perturbation Explanation states that this omission creates a potential liability risk and might violate standard labor laws requiring employers to ensure a safe working environment or compensate for damages caused to employees. The Law Explanation expands on this, referencing Article 2087 of the Italian Civil Code, which mandates employers to protect the physical integrity and moral personality of workers. This directly aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction', where the omission of essential information (mandatory insurance) creates a legal loophole that makes the contract non-compliant with laws intended to protect employee well-being. While there are no scraped law snippets provided, the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly indicate a contradiction.
